description:
You are an MLOps engineer in charge of housekeeping the experiment area of
    a research workstation.  The current directory layout is:

      /home/user/experiments          →  raw experiment folders (& files)
      /home/user/archive              →  place where compressed *.tar.gz archives must go
      /home/user/logs                 →  place where housekeeping logs must go
      /home/user/bin                  →  your personal helper-scripts

    1)  Create a Bash script called /home/user/bin/archive_experiments.sh.
        The file must be owned by the current user, be executable
        ( -rwxr-xr-x ) and start with the usual #!/usr/bin/env bash she-bang.

        Script responsibilities:
          • Find every regular file or directory directly inside
            /home/user/experiments that has a modification date
            strictly older than seven (7) days.
          • Pack all of those “old” items into
            /home/user/archive/experiments_YYYYMMDD.tar.gz  
            where YYYYMMDD is the current local date at **script runtime**.
          • After the archive is successfully created, delete the originals
            from /home/user/experiments.
          • Append one single-line log entry to
            /home/user/logs/archive_cron.log with this exact layout
               YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS - Archived <COUNT> items into <ARCHIVE_NAME> (<SIZE_BYTES> bytes)
            ‑ <COUNT> is the number of items that were added to the archive
            ‑ <ARCHIVE_NAME> is the full path of the tarball that was written
            ‑ <SIZE_BYTES> is the size (in bytes, no thousands separator) of the
              resulting tarball as reported by “stat -c %s”.

        The log line’s field separator is a single blank character, so the
        automated checker can `cut -d' '` it.

    2)  Register the script as a cron job for the current user so that it
        runs every day at 02:30 in the morning.  Use exactly this crontab
        entry (including the leading “0” in the minute field):

              30 2 * * * /home/user/bin/archive_experiments.sh

        The job must run using the default shell environment that cron
        provides, so do NOT rely on user-interactive features such as “~”.

    3)  After installing the cron line, execute the script **once** manually
        so that the first housekeeping run happens immediately and leaves
        evidence for the test suite.  Ensure the script prints a short
        human-readable summary to STDOUT; the precise content of STDOUT is
        not tested, but having any output helps debugging.

    4)  When you are done, the system must be in this state:

          a) /home/user/bin/archive_experiments.sh exists, is executable and
             matches the functional description above.
          b) The user’s crontab contains exactly one line that starts with
             “30 2 * * * /home/user/bin/archive_experiments.sh”.
          c) A tarball whose name matches the pattern
                experiments_YYYYMMDD.tar.gz
             exists inside /home/user/archive (YYYYMMDD = today).
          d) /home/user/experiments is now empty (all older test artifacts
             were removed by the script you just ran).
          e) /home/user/logs/archive_cron.log contains at least one line in
             the format specified in step 1 because you executed the script
             manually.

    NOTE:
      • Do not modify /etc/crontab or drop files into /etc/cron.*; use the
        per-user crontab that `crontab -e` manipulates.
      • Use relative times (-mtime +7) instead of calculating dates yourself.
      • The automated checker will reproduce every verification step only
        with standard POSIX tools (bash, cron, find, tar, stat, cut, grep).
truth:
Initial filesystem state created by the test harness BEFORE the agent
    starts:

      1. Directories:
          /home/user/experiments
          /home/user/archive
          /home/user/logs
          /home/user/bin

      2. Content inside /home/user/experiments
         (all items have their mtime set to exactly 10 days ago):

            /home/user/experiments/exp_alpha/model.bin
            /home/user/experiments/exp_beta/model.chk
            /home/user/experiments/exp_gamma/
                  └── metrics.json

      3. There are NO files yet in /home/user/archive or /home/user/logs.

    Verification logic (run after the agent finishes):

      • Script file:
          Path: /home/user/bin/archive_experiments.sh
          Must exist, be executable (stat mode 100755) and its first line
          must match the regex "^#!/usr/bin/env[ ]\+bash$".
          Grep will also check for: "find .* -mtime +7", "tar", "stat" and
          "echo .*Archived".

      • Cron registration:
          crontab -l 2>/dev/null | grep -F \
              "30 2 * * * /home/user/bin/archive_experiments.sh"

      • Archive file:
          TODAY=$(date +%Y%m%d)
          ARCHIVE=/home/user/archive/experiments_${TODAY}.tar.gz
          Test: [ -f "$ARCHIVE" ]

      • Experiments directory should now be empty:
          [ -z "$(ls -A /home/user/experiments)" ]

      • Log file line:
          grep -E "^[0-9]{4}-[0-9]{2}-[0-9]{2} [0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2} - Archived [0-9]+ items into /home/user/archive/experiments_${TODAY}.tar.gz \([0-9]+ bytes\)$" \
              /home/user/logs/archive_cron.log

      All five checks must pass for the task to be considered complete.
